questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: "She ___ play the piano very well."
questions.explanation: Modals never take -s or -to before the base verb: she can play. "Cans" and "can to" are both wrong.

questions.prompt: Choose the best advice: "You look tired. You ___ go to bed early."
questions.explanation: "Should" expresses advice: you should go to bed early. "Must not" and "may not" express prohibition — not advice.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Students ___ wear a uniform at school. It is the rule."
questions.explanation: "It is the rule" shows an obligation imposed from outside, which "have to" expresses: students have to wear a uniform. "should" is only advice (weaker than a rule); "might" and "could" express possibility, not obligation.

questions.prompt: Which sentence means you are NOT allowed to do something?
questions.explanation: "Mustn't" expresses prohibition — you are not allowed to smoke here. "Don't have to" simply means it is not necessary.

questions.prompt: Complete: "___ I use your dictionary, please?" (informal permission)
questions.explanation: "Can" is used for informal permission requests: Can I use your dictionary? "Could" would be more polite; "May" more formal.

questions.prompt: Complete: "I'm not sure, but she ___ be at the library."
questions.explanation: "Might" expresses weak or uncertain possibility: she might be at the library. "Must" would be near certainty; "might" fits "I'm not sure".
